title: Integrating Stream Storage and Lagging Effects in Analytical Models for Stream Depletion Prediction

research.question: How can an analytical stream-aquifer model predict pumping-induced stream depletion when stream stage responds to finite channel storage and depletion may lag the hydraulic-head difference?
research.system: An unconfined aquifer pumped by a well beside a partially penetrating rectilinear stream whose stage and stored volume can change.
research.inputData: Aquifer and stream parameters, pumping geometry, stream-depletion observations from Doyleston Drain in New Zealand and stations S1a and S2 in central Jutland, Denmark.
research.method: Couple a Dupuit-Forchheimer groundwater-flow model to a Saint-Venant-derived stream-storage boundary, introduce a lag parameter, solve semi-analytically with Laplace and Fourier cosine transforms, analyze sensitivity, and estimate parameters by least squares.
research.benchmark: The Hunt (1999) fixed-stage solution, Theis (1935), the NPS and FPS solutions of Malama et al. (2024), numerical inversion for the domain-of-validity analysis, and observed field depletion records.
research.uncertainty: Morris elementary effects quantify parameter sensitivity; residual metrics quantify field fit; relative-error maps against Hunt (1999) mark a 5% engineering-accuracy contour and field cumulative-depletion comparisons use a 20% management threshold.
research.conclusion: Finite stream storage primarily changes middle-time behavior, lagging improves field fits where depletion is asynchronous, and the fixed-stage Hunt solution remains adequate only within the paper's time- and conductance-dependent validity regimes.

narrative.throughline: A pumping well does not always receive stream water immediately or from an effectively infinite channel, so the stream boundary must become a time-dependent storage object before depletion can be predicted credibly.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that pumping lowers groundwater head and can induce leakage from a nearby stream but does not yet assume that stream stage or response timing must change.
narrative.stakes: Treating a small stream as fixed stage can overestimate short-term or long-term depletion and distort management decisions about allowable pumping.
narrative.resolution: The coupled storage-and-lag solution returns to observed stream-depletion curves and states when its extra physics materially changes the Hunt approximation.

background.premise: Fixed stream stage represents constant abundant discharge and becomes questionable during low stream flow.
background.visibleEvidence: A stream cross-section with a fixed-stage line loses water under pumping until the line can no longer remain fixed.
background.whyNow: This establishes why the stream cannot remain a passive boundary in the proposed model.
background.boundary: The concern is specific to settings where stream discharge and channel storage are not effectively infinite.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.2, paragraph preceding Equation (5)
background.sourceExcerpt: assume fixed stream stage, implying a constant and abundant stream
background.visualObject: Pumped aquifer beside a stream cross-section with a visibly falling stage line
background.motionPurpose: Lower the stage line as the pumping cone reaches the stream to expose the failed fixed-stage assumption.
background.stepDetail: Show the well drawdown cone; extend its influence to the streambed; remove water from the channel; contrast the falling stage with the former fixed line.
background.transitionBridge: The falling stream line becomes the storage state used by the coupled boundary.

background.premise: Published pumping tests report that Stenner Creek water level decreases during pumping activities.
background.visibleEvidence: A qualitative stream cross-section shows a higher stage before pumping and a lower stage during pumping.
background.whyNow: The observed stage response motivates coupling stream mass conservation to groundwater flow.
background.boundary: The Stenner Creek observation motivates the mechanism; the paper's quantitative applications use New Zealand and Denmark data.
background.sourceExcerpt: University (Cal Poly) revealed that the water level in Stenner Creek de-
background.visualObject: Qualitative before-pumping and during-pumping Stenner Creek stage states
background.motionPurpose: Lower the qualitative stream-stage state during pumping to preserve the source-reported relationship.
background.stepDetail: Show the pre-pumping stream cross-section; introduce pumping as a labeled state; lower the stream level; retain the reported qualitative relation.
background.transitionBridge: The observed stage decline becomes a changing channel-storage volume.

background.premise: An instantaneous depletion response is unrealistic when stream flow and the pumping-induced gradient are asynchronous.
background.visibleEvidence: Two time markers separate pumping onset from the first stream-depletion response.
background.whyNow: Storage alone changes magnitude but cannot represent a delayed or advanced response.
background.boundary: The lag parameter is phenomenological and may be positive or negative according to the mechanisms discussed in the paper.
background.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.6, discussion following Equation (20)
background.sourceExcerpt: However, it can lead to unrealistic result because Eq. (20) implies that the stream depletion occurs once the aquifer is pumped.
background.visualObject: Pumping pulse and stream-depletion pulse on a shared time axis with a visible offset
background.motionPurpose: Slide the depletion pulse relative to pumping to make asynchrony visible before introducing a lag symbol.
background.stepDetail: Place pumping onset; draw the instantaneous response; show its mismatch; shift the depletion response to a separate onset time.
background.transitionBridge: The time offset becomes the lag applied to the coupled boundary response.

methods.input: Three-dimensional unconfined groundwater flow around a pumped well and rectilinear stream
methods.output: Two-dimensional horizontal aquifer-flow equation in the stream-normal and stream-parallel plane
methods.validity: The paper assumes horizontal flow dominates and vertical flow components are negligible.
methods.sourceExcerpt: consistent with the assumption that horizontal flow dominates and vertical flow components are negligible.
methods.visualObject: Aquifer prism compressed vertically into a plan-view stream-well plane
methods.motionPurpose: Collapse vertical variation while preserving the stream and well positions to reveal the reduced geometry.
methods.stepDetail: Show the 3D prism; fade vertical head variation; integrate through saturated thickness; retain the horizontal plane.
methods.transitionBridge: The reduced aquifer plane receives the dynamic stream boundary in M02.

methods.input: Rectilinear stream cross-section exchanging flux with the reduced aquifer
methods.output: Time-varying stream-stage boundary without an empirical storage coefficient
methods.validity: The stream boundary follows fundamental hydraulic principles without empirical coefficients under the small channel-variation assumption.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Eq. (8) emerges directly from fundamental hydraulic principles without the introduction of empirical coefficients.
methods.visualObject: Channel control volume whose stored area shrinks as interface flux enters the aquifer
methods.motionPurpose: Convert a visible loss of channel volume into the dynamic stream-stage boundary.
methods.stepDetail: Outline the channel control volume; show interface flux; decrease stored area; attach the changing stage to the aquifer boundary.
methods.transitionBridge: The coupled physical variables are scaled into dimensionless form in M03.

methods.input: Coupled dimensional aquifer drawdown stream drawdown space time and hydraulic parameters
methods.output: Dimensionless groundwater and stream equations comparable across aquifer systems
methods.validity: The paper defines drawdown and scaling groups from pumping rate, transmissivity, storativity, distance, streambed properties, and stream storage.
methods.sourceExcerpt: These dimensionless parameters are commonly used to simplify analysis and facilitate comparison between different aquifer systems.
methods.visualObject: Dimensional stream-well geometry rescaled to unit well-stream distance and dimensionless response axes
methods.motionPurpose: Shrink units away while preserving curve shape so parameter effects can be compared on common axes.
methods.stepDetail: Mark dimensional lengths and drawdowns; divide by the paper's characteristic scales; replace axes with dimensionless coordinates.
methods.transitionBridge: The dimensionless time response is ready for transformation in M04.

methods.input: Dimensionless transient groundwater equation and boundary conditions
methods.output: Laplace-domain groundwater equation and transformed boundaries
methods.validity: The initial condition and linearized coupled equations admit the integral transform used in Section 2.5.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Applying the Laplace transform technique, Eq. (9) can be
methods.visualObject: Transient drawdown curves folded into Laplace-domain response objects
methods.motionPurpose: Replace time evolution with a transform coordinate so the differential problem becomes algebraically tractable.
methods.stepDetail: Show the transient curve; sweep across time; accumulate weighted response; place the transformed boundary beside it.
methods.transitionBridge: The remaining stream-parallel coordinate is removed in M05.

methods.input: Laplace-domain equation retaining the stream-parallel spatial coordinate
methods.output: Laplace-Fourier boundary-value problem in the stream-normal coordinate
methods.validity: Flow symmetry along the rectilinear stream supports the cosine transform.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Then, the Fourier cosine transform (based on flow symmetry in
methods.visualObject: Mirrored stream-parallel drawdown field resolving into cosine modes
methods.motionPurpose: Fold the symmetric field into cosine modes and leave a one-coordinate transformed problem.
methods.stepDetail: Mirror the field about the symmetry line; decompose the profile into cosine modes; retain the stream-normal solution track.
methods.transitionBridge: The transformed depletion relation receives the lag offset in M06.

methods.input: Instantaneous transformed relation between stream depletion and interface head difference
methods.output: Lag-adjusted stream-depletion relation allowing positive or negative response offsets
methods.validity: The paper links positive lag to baseflow or inertial effects and negative lag to microstructural resistance such as dead-end pores.
methods.sourceExcerpt: it can be modified by allowing some delays between the stream depletion and pumping
methods.visualObject: Transformed depletion pulse translated along the time axis relative to pumping
methods.motionPurpose: Move only the response timing while preserving the coupled response shape.
methods.stepDetail: Hold pumping onset fixed; translate the depletion onset; distinguish positive and negative offsets; attach the lag label to the gap.
methods.transitionBridge: The lag-adjusted relation closes the transformed boundary-value problem solved in M07.

methods.input: Laplace-Fourier groundwater equation with stream coupling lag relation and transformed boundary conditions
methods.output: Semi-analytical drawdown and stream-depletion solutions in Laplace-Fourier space
methods.validity: The appendix determines the transformed coefficients from continuity derivative jumps stream coupling and far-field decay before the physical-domain inversions are applied.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.5, Equations 18-20; PDF pages 10-11, Appendix, Equations A.1-A.4
methods.visualObject: Piecewise transformed groundwater modes joined at the stream boundaries by four coefficient conditions
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the transformed coefficients before any Fourier or Laplace coordinate is inverted.
methods.stepDetail: Place the piecewise transformed modes; impose continuity and jump conditions; solve the four coefficients; retain the Laplace-Fourier responses.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved transformed responses are ready for inverse Fourier reconstruction in M08.

methods.input: Lag-adjusted drawdown and depletion solutions in Laplace-Fourier space
methods.output: Laplace-domain aquifer drawdown and stream depletion in physical space
methods.validity: The paper evaluates the transformed solutions with an inverse Fourier cosine transformation.
methods.visualObject: Fourier modes recombining into Laplace-domain drawdown and depletion profiles across physical space
methods.motionPurpose: Reassemble the cosine modes while leaving the Laplace coordinate visibly unresolved.
methods.stepDetail: Display the transformed modes; apply the inverse cosine basis; sum the spatial contributions; retain the Laplace-domain marker.
methods.transitionBridge: The reconstructed spatial profiles still require numerical inversion from Laplace time in M09.

methods.input: Laplace-domain aquifer drawdown and stream depletion in physical space
methods.output: Aquifer drawdown and stream depletion in physical time and space
methods.validity: The paper uses the Stehfest numerical inversion scheme and implements the analyses in Python.
methods.sourceExcerpt: a numerical Laplace inversion scheme called (Stehfest, 1970)
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain spatial profiles unfolding into physical-time drawdown and depletion curves
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve the remaining transform coordinate into the observable time histories used for testing.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the spatial profiles fixed; apply the Stehfest inversion weights; advance physical time; reveal the response curves.
methods.transitionBridge: The physical curves become the output sampled during sensitivity analysis in M10.

methods.input: Physical stream-depletion predictions over sampled parameter ranges
methods.output: Elementary-effect distributions and time-dependent overall sensitivity
methods.validity: The model output is evaluated across sampled parameters through one-at-a-time perturbations that expose nonlinearity and interactions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: to quantify the impact of individual parameters on the model output.
methods.visualObject: Parameter sample cloud with one coordinate displaced and its resulting depletion-curve change
methods.motionPurpose: Move one parameter at a time and convert the response displacement into an elementary-effect distribution.
methods.stepDetail: Select a sample; perturb one coordinate; measure the curve change; repeat; assemble elementary effects into distributions.
methods.transitionBridge: Sensitivity identifies which parameters must be estimated from field curves in M11.

methods.input: Observed dimensionless stream-depletion data and model predictions
methods.output: Estimated stream storage lag and streambed parameters with residual metrics
methods.validity: The trust region reflective algorithm minimizes the squared residual sum while multiple initial guesses reduce local-minimum risk.
methods.sourceExcerpt: We employ the trust region reflective (TRF) algorithm for solving
methods.visualObject: Observed points and a model curve pulled together as the residual segments contract
methods.motionPurpose: Shorten visible residuals until the bounded optimizer settles on fitted parameters.
methods.stepDetail: Place observations; draw a trial curve; show residual segments; update parameters within the trust region; settle on the minimum.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted lag-inclusive response leaves the same observations available for an independent no-lag fit.

methods.input: Observed stream-depletion records and the proposed response constrained to zero lag
methods.output: Fitted finite-storage response and parameters without a lag offset
methods.validity: The source analyzes the same field records with the proposed solution constrained to zero lag and reports its estimates and goodness-of-fit measures separately.
methods.sourceExcerpt: both the proposed solution and the proposed solution without lagging
methods.visualObject: Observed depletion points pulling a separate zero-lag finite-storage curve toward its fitted parameters
methods.motionPurpose: Own the classical timing baseline independently before it is overlaid with lag-inclusive and Hunt responses.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the field points; set the lag offset to zero; reduce the no-lag residuals; preserve the fitted finite-storage response.
methods.transitionBridge: The site estimates now parameterize the independent fixed-stage Hunt comparator.

methods.input: Field-site aquifer streambed and pumping parameters required by the Hunt solution
methods.validity: The paper evaluates the Hunt solution with the reported site estimates and keeps its response distinct even where it overlaps the fitted no-lag curve.
methods.sourceExcerpt: solution by Hunt (1999), using the estimated results from Table 1.
methods.visualObject: Site parameter markers feeding the Hunt fixed-stage equation and releasing one comparator curve per field record
methods.motionPurpose: Produce the Hunt response before the three alternatives are compared against observations.
methods.stepDetail: Retain the site parameters; evaluate the Hunt solution through pumping time; preserve the resulting comparator curve beside the fitted responses.
methods.transitionBridge: The lag-inclusive no-lag and Hunt responses now share the observed field axes in M14.

methods.input: Fitted proposed no-lag and Hunt solutions for the New Zealand and Denmark sites
methods.output: Site-specific evidence for finite storage and lag effects
methods.validity: The same observed records and goodness-of-fit measures are used across the compared analytical solutions.
methods.locator: PDF pages 7-9, Section 3.6, Figures 8-10 and Tables 1-3
methods.sourceExcerpt: In the following analyses, we compare the proposed solution, its
methods.visualObject: Field depletion points overlaid by proposed no-lag and Hunt response curves at each site
methods.motionPurpose: Overlay the alternatives on shared observations so timing and storage improvements remain inspectable.
methods.stepDetail: Reveal field points; draw the Hunt curve; add the no-lag curve; add the lag-inclusive curve; highlight residual differences by site.
methods.transitionBridge: The observed divergences motivate an explicit map of approximation error in M15.

methods.input: Proposed and Hunt stream-depletion predictions across time and a conductance-storage parameter grid
methods.output: Time-dependent 5% validity regions for use of the fixed-stage approximation
methods.validity: The paper evaluates a defined parameter grid and uses a 5% contour as the engineering-accuracy threshold.
methods.sourceExcerpt: error in stream depletion was computed against the analytic result
methods.visualObject: Sequence of relative-error fields with a moving white 5% contour
methods.motionPurpose: Move through dimensionless time so the safe fixed-stage region visibly contracts or shifts.
methods.stepDetail: Compute paired predictions; color relative error; trace the 5% contour; advance time; classify early intermediate and late regimes.
methods.transitionBridge: The validity contour returns to the original stream-well system as a model-choice boundary.

symbols.visualObject: Vertical separation between initial and pumped aquifer head
symbols.transition: Transform the measured vertical head difference into the label s

symbols.visualObject: Vertical separation between initial and pumped stream stage
symbols.transition: Transform the falling stream-stage bracket into the label s_r

symbols.visualObject: Volumetric extraction arrow at the pumping well
symbols.transition: Attach Q to the well extraction arrow

symbols.visualObject: Flux arrow crossing the stream-aquifer interface toward the well
symbols.transition: Convert the interface-flux arrow into a time-series ordinate label

symbols.visualObject: Horizontal time gap between pumping onset and depletion response
symbols.meaning: Lag parameter controlling asynchronous stream depletion
symbols.transition: Place a brace across the visible onset offset

return.visualObject: The original stream-well cross-section paired with field depletion curves and a 5% model-choice contour
return.claim: Representing finite stream storage and lagging yields physically interpretable site parameters and modest but consistent fit improvements over Hunt (1999), while the added effects matter most in bounded time windows and smaller-stream settings.
return.claimBoundary: The rectilinear-stream analytical assumptions remain in force; near-stream points where stream width invalidates the line-source representation were excluded, anomalous field points were not resolved, and outside the mapped validity region the paper recommends Hunt or numerical inversion as appropriate.
return.sourceExcerpt: ging while delivering modest — but consistent — improvements in fit over (Hunt, 1999) solution.

visual.primaryObject: Rectilinear stream hydraulically connected to a pumped unconfined aquifer
visual.accentObject: Time-shifted stream-depletion curve and shrinking channel-storage cross-section
visual.measuredQuantity: Pumping-induced stream depletion rate and cumulative depletion volume
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the well-stream plan-view geometry continuous; transform the stream stage into stored cross-sectional area, then into depletion curves. Show lag as a horizontal offset and model validity as a white 5% contour over the conductance-storage plane.
